metadata.subject: Arts Education
metadata.description: Arts Education 9 deepens the integrated arts learning of earlier grades, focusing on identity, community, and cross-cultural insight. Students engage more deeply with the unique sensory languages of dance, drama, music, and visual arts — developing artistic technique, critical response skills, and ethical awareness. Aboriginal worldviews and diverse cultural perspectives are central to the curriculum. This course prepares students for senior specialized arts pathways.
metadata.general_description: An integrated arts course exploring identity, community, and cross-cultural understanding through dance, drama, music, and visual arts — developing artistic technique, reflective practice, and ethical responsibility in artistic creation.

big_ideas.statement: Identity is explored, expressed, and impacted through arts experiences.
big_ideas.elaboration: Participating in and creating art is a form of self-discovery and self-expression. Through artistic engagement, students develop and articulate their sense of who they are — individually and in relation to their communities and cultures.

big_ideas.statement: The arts provide opportunities to gain insight into the perspectives and experiences of people from a variety of times, places, and cultures.
big_ideas.elaboration: Engaging with artworks from diverse traditions — including Indigenous, historical, and contemporary works — builds empathy, global awareness, and appreciation for the range of human experience.

big_ideas.statement: Creative arts experiences can build community and nurture relationships with others.
big_ideas.elaboration: Collaborative artistic creation — improvising, rehearsing, performing, and creating together — cultivates mutual respect, trust, listening skills, and a sense of shared purpose.

big_ideas.statement: Dance, drama, music, and visual arts each use their own unique sensory language for creating and communicating.
big_ideas.elaboration: Each art form offers distinct expressive possibilities: kinaesthetic and spatial language in dance, dramatic tension and character in drama, sonic and rhythmic language in music, and visual language in the visual arts. Developing fluency in these languages deepens both artistic ability and communication skills.

competencies.raw: Generate artistic works individually and collaboratively using imagination, inquiry, and purposeful play; investigate materials, environments, and techniques by combining elements and processes; recognize personal, social, cultural, historical, and environmental contexts; examine identity, place, culture, and belonging; intentionally combine elements to establish specific moods or meanings.
i_can_statements: I can generate artistic works — individually and collaboratively — using imagination, inquiry, and purposeful experimentation.
I can investigate and combine artistic materials, techniques, and processes to establish specific moods and meanings.
I can recognize and reflect on the personal, social, cultural, historical, and environmental contexts that shape artistic work.
I can explore my own identity, sense of place, and cultural belonging through artistic creation.

competencies.raw: Analyze how artists employ tools, processes, technologies, and materials to express ideas; enhance ideas, processes, and technical abilities to strengthen artistic quality.
i_can_statements: I can analyze how artists — including First Peoples artists — use tools, techniques, and materials to express specific ideas.
I can refine my own artistic ideas, processes, and technical skills to strengthen the quality of my work.
I can give and receive constructive feedback to improve artistic work.

competencies.raw: Transfer learned abilities for new contexts, purposes, and audiences; compose and develop ideas using symbolism, imagery, and artistic elements; refine and document creative works to strengthen presentation or performance.
i_can_statements: I can transfer artistic skills and techniques to new contexts and audiences.
I can use symbolism, imagery, and artistic elements intentionally to develop and communicate my ideas.
I can refine and document my creative work through reflection, iteration, and formal documentation.

competencies.raw: Examine artworks and creative processes to inform personal growth; embrace creative experimentation; show regard for self, peers, and audiences; work reciprocally during artistic creation; develop meaningful artistic collections demonstrating understanding of social and cultural contexts; demonstrate increasingly sophisticated engagement with curriculum content.
i_can_statements: I can examine artworks and creative processes as a way to support my own personal and artistic growth.
I can work collaboratively with care and reciprocity, showing respect for myself, peers, and audiences.
I can develop a body of artistic work that demonstrates thoughtful engagement with social and cultural contexts.
I can incorporate First Peoples worldviews and artistic traditions into my creative thinking with respect and understanding.

areas.name: Dance
areas.raw: Dance elements, technique, movement composition, and cultural contexts.
topics: Elements of dance: body, space, time, dynamics, and relationships — with increasing complexity
Style-specific technique: contemporary, hip hop, ballroom, traditional cultural forms
Choreographic structures: ABA, rondo, theme and variation, narrative
Composition and improvisation: creating original movement phrases
First Peoples dance traditions: protocols, meaning, and respectful participation
Innovations in dance: influential choreographers and dance companies

areas.name: Drama
areas.raw: Drama conventions, performance, and theatrical contexts.
topics: Drama elements: character, conflict, tension, atmosphere, time, and place
Performance styles: naturalistic, stylized, physical theatre, commedia dell'arte
Devised theatre: creating original dramatic works from prompts, images, or issues
Script interpretation: performing scenes from diverse theatrical traditions
First Peoples storytelling: oral traditions, ceremony, and theatrical adaptations
Theatre in society: drama's role in social commentary and community healing

areas.name: Music
areas.raw: Music elements, performance, composition, and cultural traditions.
topics: Elements of music: rhythm, melody, harmony, dynamics, texture, timbre, structure
Notation: expanded reading and writing in appropriate clefs; chord notation
Performance development: technique, expression, and ensemble skills
Composition and improvisation: creating melodies, harmonizations, and rhythmic patterns
Aboriginal music traditions: instruments, songs, and protocols
World music: diverse global traditions and their cultural contexts

areas.name: Visual Arts
areas.raw: Visual arts elements, media, processes, and cultural traditions.
topics: Expanded use of elements and principles: compositional strategies and intentional design
Media and processes: expanded painting, sculpture, printmaking, ceramics, and digital art
Art history: study of significant artworks across cultures, with focus on underrepresented voices
First Peoples visual arts: design principles, cultural significance, and community protocols
Portfolio development: selecting, organizing, and reflecting on a body of artwork

areas.name: Social Responsibility and Ethics in the Arts
areas.raw: Personal and social responsibility; ethics of cultural appropriation and plagiarism.
topics: Social responsibility: artists as voices in communities and social change
Cultural appropriation: deepened analysis of power, privilege, and respect
Plagiarism and copyright in digital and arts contexts
First Peoples intellectual and cultural property: protocols and permission
Artistic integrity and personal voice
